item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The non-compliant images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the reference to Perec, I wish Calvino had something more than name-dropping. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But the number of all possible image combinations is wrong!

Assuming that the all possible colours in SCP-2334 are 224 and total pixels are 6000000, it should be (224)6000000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is strange and really bizzare, but the imagery of some pilgrims trying to find something in this endless maze of photos is fascinating. So I will upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1010^10^7 is much more than 10700.

It's 1010^10,000,000. For comparison, a googolplex is 1010^100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couldn't you just write it out as 10^700 instead of 10^10^10^7? It just looks so out of place.

Regardless, this made me think of a video I'd seen a while back: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Yy_unGaD-w

"In a gray scale image 256x256 pixels, with 256 different shades of gray, there are 2.6x10^157,826 possible images. Just for reference, since the dawn of time 10^17 seconds have passed. If you were to make a super computer that could create 10^15 different gray scale images per second and you ran it from the dawn of the universe to now, it.. wouldn't even dent it. Basically zero progress. But, then you say, "Alright, let's go again. But this time, I'll take a single atom from my universe put it in a pile." So you rinse and repeat and keep adding to the pile of atoms until you've got enough atoms to make an entirely new universe. Now, make a row of universes 1,924 long and you will have finally finished creating every possible gray scale image.
And don't even get me started on color."

You beat me to the color. Truly, an absolutely mind blowing amount of pictures. It's practically infinite, really. Every possible picture of every alien that ever existed, the death and birth of the universe, but there's also so much incoherent static. My brain literally hurts thinking about it.
Regardless, I loved it. Enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: Provenance
item.content:encoded:
Picture #1, the enormous pile of photos, I haven't found a source for.

Picture Sample #4, the key on the newspaper, is taken from Shutterstock and is not available under a license that's compatible with the SCP Wiki.

The images from room #9 and room #121 are too simple to be copyrighted.

The original of the DeepDreamed pic is at https://imgur.com/S5dpXp0 ; as can be seen here - https://mlconf.com/mlconf-2017-new-york-city/#ross - it's a picture of technologist Ross Goodwin. I see no indication that it's available under a compatible license.

The spooky photo is based on http://www.old-picture.com/indians/Indian-Costume.htm - described as "Nayenezgani, a Navajo Indian", wearing "an authentic Navajo Ceremonial Costume" made of "dark leather mask, fur ruff, cloth girdle, silver concho belt and necklaces". Photo was taken in 1904, by Edward S. Curtis, and is public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This and SCP-2750 share the same image of that masked thing. Is there a possibility that both skips are connected?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do think that this thing could maybe be Euclid, instead of Safe, especially because of SCP-096. Since this has every possible photograph, and and even 4 pixels can trigger the reaction of 096, well, there's just the possibility for this to go very, very, wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd hate to be the first researcher to find a picture of 096 in there. Or the second, or third…

On a more serious note though, aren't the containment procedures on this way too lax considering how much vital information is in here and the possibility that researchers could find cognitohazards or possibly breach containment of photograph related scips?

The concept is interesting enough though, so I'll no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this one was a little weird, having actually been in the room with the piece depicted in that first photo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there anything to the picture from Room 121, because I've been looking awhile and see nothing but static.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"These copies appeared to be photographs of official Foundation documents, albeit with text altered to avoid use of the letter 'e'.

I'm not quite sure what this has to do with anything or have I missed something important? I feel as though that is completely unneeded and can be omitted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The story about that place that holds all the possible books? I have no idea where I heard about it, but that sounds kinda familiar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's just a bunch of pictures? The concept's somewhat interesting, but I didn't find it entertaining. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep staring at it to be sure…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm just an idiot catching on to what everyone already knew, but is the image behind Room 121 a magic eye puzzle or have I just been indoctrinated to this cult by staring at it for too long?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except the Foundation is likely to use instruments to determine the actual, not perceived, pixel depths. While your eye can possibly see at 24 bits of RBG, mine might see more or less using more or less cones depending on age, damage, genetics, etc.

Also, I think there was an article recently about some people seeing more colors because they're mutant freaks. Really. That's assuming they've decided the paper isn't reflecting unusual infrared/ultraviolet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
10 10 10^7

,,10,, 10 ^^10^7^^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a couple little picky things for the sake of polish.

… a religious sect of students …

A sect is usually a subset of a religious group, not a religious subset of any group. The more academically sound term here is NRM (New Religious Movement).

… religious group known as Monks of the Archive …

Same note on the NRM replacement. Also, I love the idea of this group, which is why I'm really struggling with the name "Monks of the Archive"; it's very underwhelming for something this interesting and almost smacks of the Library a little more. Maybe "Seekers in the Noise" or "Resolutionists" or something with a little more flair?

Anyway, all-in-all, I'm a big fan. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the best of my knowledge, even countries that are on the metric standard use dots per inch (DPI) to measure photographic resolution, and most color photographs are printed at around 300dpi; I would probably change the line to something like "printed at 20cm on the longest side at a resolution of 300dpi," which are statistics I just grabbed off of a European photo site .

It should also be mentioned that printed photographs don't use red/green/blue (RGB) colors for printing; this is specific to images that use emitted light, such as computer screens, and not to items that reflect light, such as printed photographs. Modern printers use cyan/magenta/yellow/key colors (CMYK, where key is typically black), which is sometimes called "process" or "four color process" printing. Here's a link to the wikipedia article on color printing.

Good catch on that most of the photographs would appear to be static. I wonder if it would be worth mentioning, since Foundation documents appear to have been photographed, that a small number of photographs would contain mimetic/info/cognitohazards, many of which would be unknown to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the actual pixels themselves are important to the anomaly here, the resolution does matter, but luckily there is a solution.

A crash course on printing resolutions
Warning:Potentially migrane inducing

You might need to adjust your extremely large number a bit, but this should solve your problem.

First off, image printing measurements are almost always based on inches and DPI.1

The standard print sizes for 35mm film are 4x6 inches (the name gets rounded to "10 x 15 cm" internationally) and 8x12 ("20 x 30 cm").

The standard resolution for a quality print of a digital image is 300 Dots Per Inch (DPI). This means that the sets of red green and blue dots that make up a digital pixel are printed so that 3002 equivalent ink dots fit in one square inch. If the pixels are in terms of DPI, a 4x6 print needs to be 1200 × 1800 px for good quality (300DPI) and 2400 x 3600 px for really good quality (600DPI). For an 8x12, you'd need 2400x3600 or 4800x7200 px respectively.

There is also an alternative color mixing system called halftone color (aka CMYK), which might be a better fit this anomaly: This is the system most printers both private and industrial actually use, which combines cyan, magenta, yellow, and black dots of various sizes to create color. This would alter your calculation for all possible combinations because CMYK has four variables rather than three, but it would also fix the fact that RGB color does not work directly in print or pigment color (for instance, you can't make black or white with Red Green and Blue pigment, unlike with red, green, and blue light).

Additionally, halftone has a better density-to-quality ratio (because of its overlapping dots) that has to be factored in: a CMYK print at 300 LPI has equivalent quality to 600 DPI in RGB. A photo-quality 4x6 image would have thus have only 4x1200x1800 = 8640000 variables as opposed to the 18000000 of an image of the same quality and printed size described in RGB… and you can do the math for the number of possible combinations if you want to go with that option. X3

Footnotes
1. yes even internationally- DPI standards get really ugly when converted to CM, and lots of very old and expensive printing machines make us keep using them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More than that. 24-bit colours which IIRC are commonly used because they give effectively as much image quality as the human eye cares for have acceptable values for R, G and B coefficients being 0-255.
In other words, you have a set of 2000*3000 pixels, each coloured one of 2^24 colours.
Which would I think be best approximated by P(2000*3000*2^24,2000*3000) - in other words, choosing six million elements without repetition, in some order, from a set of six-million-bricks sets of every colour possible (roughly 16 million)

Which makes n!/(n-k)! = (6 000 000 * 16 000 000)!/(6 000 000 * 15 999 999)!
For these sorts of numbers, n! can be approximated as (n/e)^n where n is the basis of natural logarithm. Since this is far too stupid to count with, let's calculate the decimal logarithm by logging the whole equation… we'll get n ln (n/e) and so on…

The resulting answer is of the order of 10^(80 000 000)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured the resolution was important to the math for the number of possible images. That is, 2000x3000 pixels, with each pixel every possible value of RGB, gives you a probability equation for number of possible images.

So, what, 3^(2000*3000) ? Unless I'm misunderstanding RGB, which is very possible.
Edit: oh, it's right there in the article XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless the numbers themselves are significant, the resolution of the scanned images is pretty much irrelevant. You can scan in an image at just about any resolution you want. Now, if that resolution is part of the anomaly, that'd be a whole different matter. In any case, if I were writing this article, I would include the dimensions of the printed images. It's a detail that lends to verisimilitude but it's not critical to the understanding and story of the scip. I like the article as is, that's just the one thing that jumped out at me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I add that it's the digital resolution of scanned images and provide dimensions for the prints, does that work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops! Well, that's embarrassing. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As the author of SCP-1986 I agree. In a good way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article, upvoted but with one minor quibble…

printed at an approximate resolution of 2000 by 3000 pixels

This is largely meaningless for a printed image. Print resolution is measured in DPI (dots per inch) rather than pixels, as you can often have a great many more dots per inch than you can pixels on a screen for similar sizes. You can print an 8x10 image at 1200DPI. That same image, at that resolution, measured in pixels would be 9600x12000. So, saying the images are 2000x3000 doesn't really tell us at all how big each picture is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of SCP-1986, in a good way. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch! Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the 'the' in the spatial control paradigm intentional? I assume that it wasn't supposed to have any 'e's whatsoever. If that's the case, you could use 'said artifact' instead of 'the artifact'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it's this many:
http://www.wolframalpha.com/input/?i=10%5E10%5E10%5E7+%2F+10%5E10%5E100

Not sure if that's the same as 100,000.

I need to fix the exponent notation to make it more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed. Looking back, a Library of Babel but for photos is such an obvious expansion of the concept that it's hard to believe nobody thought of it before.
Also, props for a good use of a deep-dreamed pic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really. Looks to me like the spooky cult formed after the SCP came into being, and was inspired by it. That's at least a bit different from the usual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, salt mines are prime real estate for document storage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how completely meaningless that number is. It's just so big! I also like the weirdness of this photo cult, and "SCP-2334 is an anomalous underground salt mine and photograph storage facility", as if those two things were meant to go together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most likely. 1010^10^7 is 1010^(10^7), or 100,000 googolplexes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's super-super script for stacked exponents. Supposed to look like this: http://www.wolframalpha.com/input/?i=2%5E24%5E6000000

If anyone knows of a way to do that using wikidot syntax, please let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The nested exponentials seem odd to me. Is that a normal format that I have just never seen before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the initial concept of every photo ever interests me, you took the SCP to the least interesting and safest path: generic spooky cult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The total number of images possible at 2000 by 3000 pixels (the approximate resolution of each print) is calculated to be 224^6000000, or nearly 1010^10^7.
Footnotes
1. The number of atoms in the observable universe is believed to be approximately 1080.

I'm glad you included this; I wouldn't have gotten the significance of the numbers otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. Still deciding what to think about it. However, one note - it's spelled "spatial", not "spacial". (I know, given that the root word is "space", you'd think that would have a "c" in it, but what can I say, that's English for ya.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, agreed. Edits made, though I compromised on the footnote because some folks liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. That line is narmy as all hell.

I say drop the footnote. The Foundation has found and contains many things that clearly prove everything we know wrong; a vault with literally ever photograph possible is par for the course for them, and having m more photos than atoms in the universe wouldn't come as a total shock besides maybe to the newbies. Or maybe I'm just jaded, idunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a major fan of Jorge Luis Borges, I am compelled to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My eyes burn from the static. It cleanses! Oh, how it cleanses!

I think this is just a bit over the top for me. +1 anyways.
